Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines `pkgver`, `pkgrel`, `source`, and a `package()` function. The `source` array downloads two `.dat` files from the official GitHub releases of the upstream project (`https://github.com/Loyalsoldier/v2ray-rules-dat`). Checksums are pinned and not SKIP. There is no top-level code execution (no commands or command substitutions outside functions), no `pkgver()` function, and no obfuscated or dangerous code. The `package()` function runs only during packaging, which is not invoked by `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare`. Therefore, running the two commands is safe.
</details>
<summary>Standard PKGBUILD with pinned checksums; no dangerous code in scope.</summary>
</security_assessment>

<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file is a `geosite.dat` database, a standard binary data file used by v2ray/Xray core for domain-based routing. The truncated content shows domain names and categorization metadata (e.g., `CATEGORY-ADS`, `CATEGORY-EMBY`, `TENCENT`). No suspicious commands (`curl`, `wget`, `eval`, `base64`, `exec`) are present, and there is no obfuscated code, network exfiltration attempts, or system modification instructions. The file is a static data asset, not an executable script. All observed domain entries are standard and belong to known services or categories. No evidence of a supply-chain attack or malicious injection.
</details>
<summary>Legitimate geosite.dat domain list, no malicious code.</summary>

LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
This PKGBUILD is a standard Arch Linux package definition for v2ray-rules-dat. It downloads two pre-built data files (geoip.dat and geosite.dat) from the official GitHub releases of the project author (Loyalsoldier) using pinned URLs with version tags. SHA-256 checksums are provided for integrity verification. The package() function simply installs these files into the appropriate system directories for v2ray and xray. There are no unexpected network requests, no obfuscation, no execution of downloaded code, no exfiltration, and no tampering with system files outside the package's scope. All actions are consistent with normal, safe packaging practices for distributing pre-compiled rule data.
</details>
<summary>Standard PKGBUILD with no malicious behavior.</summary>
</security_assessment>

audits.body:
Cloning https://aur.archlinux.org/v2ray-rules-dat.git to /tmp/aur-sleuth/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-vxg3_qi5...
Cloned repository to /tmp/aur-sleuth/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-vxg3_qi5
Analyzing v2ray-rules-dat AUR package (working in /tmp/aur-sleuth/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-vxg3_qi5) with qwen/qwen3-235b-a22b-2507 from https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines a source array that fetches two .dat files from GitHub Releases using predictable URLs based on `pkgver`. The URLs are constructed using the `_author` variable and the package's own `pkgver`, both of which are hardcoded or declared within the PKGBUILD itself. The sources are downloaded directly over HTTPS from GitHub (github.com), which is the expected upstream for this package.

During `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare`, the only executed code outside of `package()` is the global scope and any `pkgver()` function (which is absent here). There is no `pkgver()` function, so no dynamic version-fetching logic executes. All variables are statically defined, and there are no command substitutions, `eval`s, or function calls in the top-level scope that could lead to code execution or data exfiltration. The `sha256sums` are provided and valid, but even if they were `SKIP`, it would be acceptable for VCS or release-binary sources.

The sources are binary data files (geoip.dat, geosite.dat) commonly used by V2Ray/Xray for routing decisions. Fetching them during the source retrieval phase poses no risk, as no build or prepare steps are run under the current command flags. The `package()` function is not executed during this stage, so its contents are irrelevant for this safety gate.
</details>
<summary>
No risky code execution during source fetch
</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- No risky code execution during source fetch[/]
Running makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare to download sources for agent...
makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare successful
Running makepkg --printsrcinfo to get source files for agent...
makepkg --printsrcinfo successful
Reviewing required files...
[0/2] Reviewing geosite-202606182340.dat...
[0/2] Reviewing geosite-202606182340.dat, PKGBUILD...
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD is for the `v2ray-rules-dat` package, which provides pre-built `.dat` files (geoip and geosite databases) used by V2Ray and XRay. The source URLs point directly to GitHub releases under the trusted `_author=Loyalsoldier` organization, which maintains these rule sets. The files are fetched using predictable, versioned release URLs, and their integrity is verified via SHA-256 checksums that match the expected values.

The `package()` function safely copies the downloaded `.dat` files into the appropriate system directories under `/usr/share/v2ray` and `/usr/share/xray`. No network operations, downloads, or script executions occur during build or install phases beyond what is declared in `source`. There is no use of dangerous functions like `eval`, no obfuscation, and no unexpected system modifications. The `provides` and `conflicts` arrays are standard for this type of rule-file package to avoid conflicts with alternatives.

Checksums are provided and match the release artifacts. The use of GitHub releases (as opposed to cloning a git repo without pinning) ensures immutability. No AUR helper scripts or post-install hooks perform out-of-scope actions. All behavior is consistent with a static data file package.
</details>
<summary>
Safe: standard data package with verified sources
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[1/2] Reviewing geosite-202606182340.dat...
+ Reviewed PKGBUILD. Status: SAFE -- Safe: standard data package with verified sources
LLM auditresponse for geosite-202606182340.dat:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided file is a binary data file named `geosite-202606182340.dat`, which appears to be a geolocation-based domain categorization database used by tools like V2Ray or similar traffic routing software. It contains structured lists of domains grouped under categories such as `CATEGORY-ADS`, `BILIBILI2`, `CHINAPOST`, `TENCENT`, and others, primarily for the purpose of filtering or directing network traffic based on domain patterns.

Analysis of the file content—including head, tail, and any lines containing potentially suspicious patterns (e.g., `curl`, `wget`, `eval`, `base64`, `exec`)—reveals **no executable code**, **no command injection**, and **no dynamic payloads**. The data is in a structured binary/proto-like format consisting of domain names and category identifiers, with no evidence of obfuscation, unauthorized network callbacks, or malicious logic. Domains listed are typically associated with advertising, content delivery networks (CDNs), or well-known service providers (e.g., Tencent, Google, Bilibili), consistent with standard usage in geosite datasets.

While some domains may appear sensitive (e.g., analytics or ad-tracking services), their presence is expected in this type of dataset, which is designed to identify and potentially block or route such traffic. There is no indication that the file itself performs any action beyond static data storage.

Thus, despite its large size and inclusion of many third-party domains, the file does not pose a supply-chain threat as it contains only passive data with no executable or script-like behavior.
</details>
<summary>
Binary geosite data, no executable content
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[2/2] Reviewing ...

API Usage Summary
  Models: qwen/qwen3-235b-a22b-2507
  Prompt Tokens: 27,537
  Completion Tokens: 994
  Total Tokens: 28,531
  Total Cost: $0.002578
  Execution Time: 79.14 seconds
Full audit report can be found in /tmp/aur-sleuth/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-report-v2ray-rules-dat.txt
Final Status: SAFE


No issues found.

data.reasoning: This is a simple data package that downloads two pre-built .dat files (geoip and geosite) from the official GitHub releases of the upstream maintainer (Loyalsoldier), pins SHA-256 checksums, and the package() function merely installs them into /usr/share/v2ray and /usr/share/xray. There is no pkgver() function, no top-level code execution, no build/prepare/check functions, and no .install hook. The .dat files are passive binary data with no executable content. Both models independently reached the same conclusion with accurate reasoning.
coverage_issues: Both models reviewed only PKGBUILD and one of the two .dat files (geosite). The geoip .dat file was not explicitly reviewed, though it is the same class of passive binary data and verified by pinned checksum, so the gap is low-risk.
Neither model explicitly confirmed the absence of a .install hook, though the package structure strongly implies none exists.
learnings: For data-only packages with pinned checksums and no build logic, two-file review is adequate; the 'shallow' trigger is a false alarm here since the package legitimately contains few security-relevant files.
Models correctly identified that pinned checksums on GitHub release artifacts mitigate supply-chain risk and that package() does not execute under makepkg's source-fetch gate.

The audit tool could special-case 'data dat/db file' packages to suppress shallow-review escalation when (a) PKGBUILD has no build/prepare/check/pkgver functions, (b) all sources are checksum-pinned, and (c) no .install hook is present.

Both models reasoned accurately and concisely; no prompt quality issues observed. Reviewing the second identical .dat (geoip) would have added marginal coverage with negligible additional risk insight.
